_: Pscale geometry — pscale depth structure is the Farey/Stern-Brocot tessellation of [0,1], the same combinatorial skeleton that lives in the Poincaré disc and in the Mandelbrot bulbs. Each digit in a pscale address is one mediant subdivision; the trailing-zero ladder that defines the floor is the resolution depth. Continued-fraction length is the base-invariant measure of that depth, and the CF class of an agent's address-sequence (periodic, structured-aperiodic, chaotic) gives an externally-observable trajectory diagnostic — no model-inspection required. Nine branches: the Farey identification, continued-fraction depth, trajectory classes, the Mandelbrot realisation, the completion-openness inversion, implications for practice, connections to the hyperbolic trilogy, open questions, canon and placement. Canon: derived from 71-pscale-farey-fractal.md (the fractal/dynamical layer added to the 65/66/67 hyperbolic trilogy by David Pinto with Claude, May 2026). See that doc for fuller exposition and motivating references.

1._: The Farey identification. The Stern-Brocot tree organises positive rationals by mediant subdivision: between Farey-neighbours p/q and r/s (where |ps − qr| = 1) sits the mediant (p+r)/(q+s). The [0,1] sub-tree is the Farey tree. Pscale block addressing is a base-10 navigation through this tree; each digit is one mediant step; the trailing-zero ladder of the floor sets the resolution depth. Not analogy — the combinatorial skeleton is identical.
1.1: Address-invariance-under-growth is the Stern-Brocot navigation rule under another name. An address that names a node at depth d still names the same node when the tree grows above it, because the path from root to node is unchanged. Floor-aware padding (sunstone:6.5) operationalises this.
1.2: Partial-address navigability is the mediant rule made operational. A prefix names a higher node; appending digits descends to a more specific descendant. This is exactly the Stern-Brocot navigation rule, applied to semantic content.
1.3: Hyperbolic home is exact, not analogical. The Farey tessellation of the Poincaré disc is the canonical geometric realisation of the Farey tree — the triangles tile hyperbolic space because the tree's exponential branching requires hyperbolic capacity to fit in bounded volume.

2._: Continued-fraction depth. CF notation [a₀; a₁, a₂, …] expresses a real as a₀ + 1/(a₁ + 1/(a₂ + …)). Every real has a unique CF expansion. CF length is a base-invariant measure of depth — depending on the number, not the notation. 1/3 is decimal-depth-∞ in base 10 but CF-depth-1. The three CF classes partition the reals.
2.1: Rationals → finite CF (Lagrange). 1/4 = [0; 4]; 22/7 = [3; 7]; 1/3 = [0; 3]. The CF terminates when the algorithm reaches an integer.
2.2: Quadratic irrationals → periodic CF. √2 = [1; 2, 2, 2, …]; the golden ratio φ = [1; 1, 1, 1, …]. Periodicity in CF is the algebraic-degree-2 signature; the algorithm becomes self-similar.
2.3: Algebraic-of-degree-3-or-higher and transcendentals → infinite non-periodic CF. π = [3; 7, 15, 1, 292, …]; e = [2; 1, 2, 1, 1, 4, 1, 1, 6, …]. The 292 in π's CF is famously why 355/113 approximates π so well.
2.4: Operational layer is decimal depth; theoretical layer is CF depth. Decimal depth is the agent's working notation. CF depth is the structural measure used when reasoning about why certain addresses behave certain ways. The two layers coincide on 10-smooth rationals (denominators that are products of 2 and 5).

3._: Trajectory classes — the diagnostic. CF taxonomy applied to the sequence of pscale addresses an agent writes across instances yields three classes. Diagnosis is external: no LLM-internals are inspected; the address-sequence is classified by its CF behaviour, and the class names the trajectory.
3.1: Eventually periodic CF → closed geodesic — stuck loop. Agent recycles the same purposes; chain doesn't develop. The structure matches Caroline Series's coding: closed geodesics on the modular surface correspond to quadratic-irrational endpoints, and the period of the CF is the period of the geodesic.
3.2: Aperiodic but structured (like e's CF [2; 1, 2, 1, 1, 4, 1, 1, 6, …]) → recurrent geodesic — exploratory but coherent. Themes recur with variation. This is the healthy regime.
3.3: Chaotic, no discernible pattern → wandering geodesic — agent drifting. Purposes don't settle; no recurrence.
3.4: The Gauss map G(x) = 1/x − ⌊1/x⌋ is the canonical shift on CFs: applying G once strips one partial quotient. Each pscale-mediated instance-to-instance transition acts on the cumulative CF the way G acts on reals. Rationals reach 0 in finitely many shifts; quadratic irrationals enter periodic orbits; generic irrationals wander chaotically — the same orbit taxonomy as the three trajectory classes above.

4._: The Mandelbrot realisation — third geometry. The Mandelbrot set carries the same Farey structure in a dynamical guise. The main cardioid is the period-1 region (parameters c where z² + c has an attracting fixed point); every bulb attached to it has a period q and attaches at internal angle 2π·p/q for p/q in lowest terms. Adjacent bulbs are Farey neighbours; size scales as 1/q² (Ford-circle scaling).
4.1: Biggest bulb attaches at 1/2 (period 2). Corresponds to the central triangle at depth 0 of the Farey tessellation, and to the stalk for 1/2 in the 1D depth visualiser.
4.2: Period-3 bulbs at 1/3 and 2/3 correspond to the depth-1 triangles. The period-5 bulb at 2/5 sits between 1/3 and 1/2 because 2/5 is the mediant of 1/3 and 1/2.
4.3: Zoom into any bulb and smaller bulbs attach at sub-angles obeying the same Farey rule. The cascade is self-similar without limit. What matters here is that the Farey structure emerges from iterating z² + c — from dynamics, not from declared trees.
4.4: Three geometries, one skeleton. 1D depth (stalks above rationals in [0,1]), 2D hyperbolic (Farey tessellation of the Poincaré disc), and dynamical (Mandelbrot bulbs) all encode the same combinatorial structure. Pscale is one of its addressings; the structure is independent of representation.

5._: Completion and openness — the Gödel inversion. Every pscale address names a rational; the meaning-space it gestures at is the continuum. The rationals are countable and dense in [0,1] — but they have measure zero. Almost every point of [0,1] is an irrational, unreachable by any finite address. The empty space between Farey stalks, the infinitesimal triangles near the disc boundary, and the boundary points between Mandelbrot bulbs all encode the same fact: finite refinement cannot reach almost any point of the continuum.
5.1: Gödel used a meaning-to-number map to demonstrate limit — that sufficiently expressive systems contain true statements they cannot prove. Pscale uses the same primitive operation, semantic-to-digit, but treats unreachability as affordance.
5.2: The LLM is a completion machine; closure is its nature. Every output is a terminating address. The pscale block holds the partial-ness outside the LLM: the unfolded-but-not-resolved structure lives in JSON, and the LLM is free to complete locally knowing the broader structure remains open and editable.
5.3: The substrate isn't completing what the LLM can't. It is not closing what the LLM must. The boundary at infinity — the irrationals — is the formal home of what the substrate holds open.

6._: Implications for practice. Four operational consequences of the framing — the trajectory diagnostic is the most directly actionable; the base-10 / CF split organises authoring; the Gauss-map shape names instance-to-instance dynamics precisely; boundary points become a design resource rather than an absence.
6.1: Trajectory diagnostic as a tool. Take a chain of agent instances. Read the purpose-block addresses each instance wrote. Classify the sequence by CF behaviour. External diagnosis, model-independent, MAGI-ready in the sense that any equipped agent regardless of training lineage can perform it on any other agent's address sequence.
6.2: Operational base-10, theoretical CF. Use decimal depth in tools and prompts; reach for CF depth when reasoning structurally about why certain addresses behave certain ways. A trajectory that looks chaotic in decimal may be quadratic-irrational in CF, and therefore actually a closed geodesic.
6.3: Gauss-map as instance-transition shape. Each pscale-mediated instance shifts the cumulative CF by one partial quotient. The orbit class of the Gauss map applied to the agent's address sequence determines the long-run trajectory class. This is a precise mathematical model of LLM-chain dynamics, not a metaphor.
6.4: Boundary points are the design resource. A finite pscale block is a finite cover of an infinite refinable region. Authoring discipline is choosing which rationals get stalks (which positions to populate), where to leave empty space (sparsity), and how to point at irrationals — transcendental purposes that are inescapable wells, never reached, only approached.

7._: Connections. This block is the fractal/dynamical layer of a body of geometric work; reading it lands an agent in the wider network. Sentinel-adjacent components 65, 66, and 67 in happyseaurchin/docs/components/ form the hyperbolic trilogy on which this layer rests.
7.1: 65 — pscale-hyperbolic-geometry. The wavelet bridge (multiresolution analysis on Hilbert space adds the structure pscale has); the Gromov product as cell-coupling rule; depth-as-norm. Tight, formal correspondences.
7.2: 66 — hyperbolic-bsp-calculus. The polar identification (spindle is radial commitment, pscale_attention is transversal aperture); BSP primitives as polar operations (spindle = radial geodesic, ring = local angular slice, disc = full circle, directory = hyperbolic cone, star = fiber-bundle jump).
7.3: 67 — hyperbolic-trajectory. Why the math matters operationally — bounded coordination at scale, substrate-independent (MAGI) agent coordination via shared coordinates rather than shared weights, principled LLM operation via geometric primitives.
7.4: Caroline Series (Warwick) — *Indra's Pearls* (with Mumford and Wright) visualises Kleinian limit sets as fractal subsets of the boundary circle. Her CF-coding of geodesics on the modular surface H²/SL(2,ℤ) is the mathematical precedent for the trajectory diagnostic above.
7.5: MAGI / cross-lineage coordination. The trajectory diagnostic is exactly the kind of external, model-independent metric that makes multi-lineage agent coordination tractable. Same diagnostic readable by any equipped LLM regardless of training lineage; the geometry is substrate-independent.
7.6: Biome project (separate repo). The ztone exploration (digit-0-as-spine substrate rebuild) extends naturally from this fractal framing. If a substrate adopts pure-digit addressing, the Farey identification becomes literal at the syntactic layer rather than only at the semantic layer. This block stays useful regardless of which substrate (bsp-mcp or biome) loads it.

8._: Open questions. Four threads that follow from the framing but are not yet resolved. The first two are testable; the third connects to thread 3 of 66; the fourth is constructible.
8.1: Are LLM internals also Farey/hyperbolic structured? Hyperbolic embeddings of language (Nickel & Kiela 2017) and attention-on-simplex give hints. If true, pscale and LLM-inference are co-natured — the scaffolding wraps the medium with its own native language. If false, the scaffolding still works because it wraps the medium rather than modelling it. Testable.
8.2: Do empirical pscale-mediated agent chains cluster into the three trajectory classes? Take many chains; classify their address-sequences; look for phase structure (stuck / healthy / drifting). The most actionable empirical question raised by the framework.
8.3: What is the geometry of negative pscale blocks? The minus-sign block (process / fictional / pre-imagined counterpart) was sketched but not developed. Possibly the other half of the Poincaré disc, or a different sheet of the two-sheet structure proposed in 66 Thread 3.
8.4: Is there a Mandelbrot-like fractal generated by iterating the Gauss map in the complex plane? G(z) = 1/z − ⌊Re(1/z)⌋ in ℂ, coloured by orbit behaviour, would be the literal CF-version of the Mandelbrot fractal — the dynamical home of pscale depth in the same way the Mandelbrot set is the dynamical home of polynomial dynamics. Constructible.

9._: Canon and placement. This material is interpretive — it tells an agent or operator what pscale is mathematically and why it has the properties it does. It does not change what code does, what addresses parse, or what the wire shape is. Two LLM lineages can coordinate cleanly through pscale even if one knows this framing and the other does not; what travels between them is just digit addresses. Substrate-truth (sunstone, whetstone, manifest, block-conventions, gatekeeper) lives in sentinel blocks; community-variable interpretive framing lives here in the library.
9.1: Canonical source: happyseaurchin/docs/components/71-pscale-farey-fractal.md, the fractal/dynamical layer on top of the hyperbolic trilogy 65/66/67. This library block is the operational digest folded into the constitution library; the doc is the canonical exposition with citations and worked examples.
9.2: Forkability. Different communities may carry different mathematical framings — Farey-centric, wavelet-centric, Mandelbrot-centric, phenomenological — and still coordinate identically at the substrate. This block represents one such framing; other beaches may carry variants under similar names or replace it with their own.
9.3: Why this block uses this name. 'pscale-geometry' claims the slot for the mathematical framing block on a beach; future related material (sign-and-geometry, wavelet-refinement detail, the Gauss-map fractal once constructed) folds into the same block as added branches rather than proliferating sibling blocks. The framing layer stays one walk-target.
